annotations.label: Chain rule (Lagrange notation)

annotations.note: The one-variable chain rule (f∘g)' = f'(g)·g' is `HasDerivAt.comp` in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: The chain rule may also be expressed in Leibniz's notation

annotations.note: Same content as the Lagrange-notation chain rule; `deriv_comp` gives the functional form dz/dx = (dz/dy)(dy/dx).

annotations.label: Intuitive product-of-rates statement

anchor.snippet: the chain rule states that knowing the instantaneous rate of change

annotations.note: The informal product-of-rates statement is the same theorem as `HasDerivAt.comp`.

annotations.label: Car, bicycle, walking man

anchor.snippet: If a car travels twice as fast as a bicycle

annotations.note: An informal narrative example with no mathematical statement to formalize.

annotations.label: Chain rule (one variable)

anchor.snippet: if g is a function that is differentiable at a point c

annotations.note: The one-variable chain rule at a point is `HasDerivAt.comp`.

annotations.label: Chain rule for n-fold composition

anchor.snippet: Carrying the same reasoning further, given n functions

annotations.note: Mathlib has the binary chain rule; the n-fold version follows by iteration but is not stated as a separate lemma.

annotations.label: Arbitrarily long composition chain rule

anchor.section: Composites of more than two functions
anchor.snippet: it is necessary to differentiate an arbitrarily long composition

annotations.note: Iterating `HasDerivAt.comp` covers arbitrarily long chains but no explicit `List`/`Finset`-indexed lemma exists.

annotations.label: Quotient rule from chain rule

anchor.snippet: the quotient rule is a consequence of the chain rule and the product rule

annotations.note: The quotient rule is stated in Mathlib as `HasDerivAt.div` / `deriv_div`.

annotations.label: Derivative of inverse function

anchor.snippet: There is a formula for the derivative of f in terms of the derivative of g

annotations.note: The inverse-function derivative formula is `HasDerivAt.of_local_left_inverse`.

annotations.label: Inverse of exponential is logarithm

anchor.snippet: consider the function g ( x ) = e x

annotations.note: The derivative `log x = 1/x` obtained as inverse of exp is `Real.hasDerivAt_log`.

annotations.label: Failure of inverse formula at cube root

anchor.snippet: This formula can fail when one of these conditions is not true

annotations.note: No specific Mathlib lemma about the failure of the inverse derivative at the cube-root example.

annotations.label: Chain rule and backpropagation

anchor.snippet: The chain rule forms the basis of the backpropagation algorithm

annotations.note: Backpropagation as an algorithm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Faà di Bruno's formula

anchor.snippet: Faà di Bruno's formula generalizes the chain rule to higher derivatives

annotations.note: Faà di Bruno is `HasFTaylorSeriesUpToOn.comp` via `FormalMultilinearSeries.taylorComp`.

annotations.label: First proof of chain rule

anchor.snippet: One proof of the chain rule begins by defining the derivative

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the same chain-rule statement (`HasDerivAt.comp`); the article's specific proof presentation is not separately recorded.

annotations.label: Equivalent definition of differentiability

anchor.snippet: the following equivalent definition of differentiability at a point

annotations.note: The little-o form of differentiability is the very definition `HasFDerivAt`/`HasDerivAt` uses.

annotations.label: Second proof of chain rule

anchor.snippet: Another way of proving the chain rule is to measure the error

annotations.note: The error-term proof corresponds to Mathlib's proof of `HasFDerivAt.comp`, which is the chain rule itself.

annotations.label: Carathéodory differentiability

anchor.snippet: Under this definition, a function f is differentiable at a point a

annotations.note: Carathéodory's continuous-factor formulation of the derivative is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Third proof of chain rule (Carathéodory)

anchor.snippet: alternative definition of the differentiability of a function can be used to give an elegant proof

annotations.note: Statement is the chain rule (formalized), but the specific Carathéodory-style proof is not in Mathlib.

annotations.note: The multivariate scalar chain rule is the specialization of `HasFDerivAt.comp` / `fderiv_comp`.

annotations.label: Chain rule for addition

anchor.snippet: If the function f is addition

annotations.note: The sum rule (f+g)' = f'+g' is `HasDerivAt.add` (and `HasFDerivAt.add`).

annotations.label: Chain rule for multiplication

anchor.snippet: For multiplication

annotations.note: The product rule is Mathlib's `HasDerivAt.mul`.

annotations.label: Chain rule for exponentiation

anchor.snippet: The case of exponentiation

annotations.note: The chain rule applied to exp is captured via `Real.hasDerivAt_exp` composed using `HasDerivAt.comp`.

annotations.label: Chain rule for total derivatives

anchor.snippet: The chain rule for total derivatives is that their composite is the total derivative

annotations.note: The total-derivative chain rule is exactly `HasFDerivAt.comp`.

annotations.label: Jacobian of composite is product of Jacobians

anchor.snippet: the Jacobian of a composite function is the product of the Jacobians

annotations.note: `fderiv_comp` expresses the Fréchet derivative of a composite as the composition of the linear maps (Jacobians).

annotations.label: Chain rule for partial derivatives

anchor.snippet: The chain rule for total derivatives implies a chain rule for partial derivatives

annotations.note: Mathlib gives the total-derivative chain rule; partial-derivative form follows but isn't stated as a single explicit lemma.

annotations.label: Partial derivatives via chain rule

anchor.snippet: determine the value of

annotations.note: A worked numerical example with no corresponding Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Faà di Bruno in multivariable case

anchor.snippet: Faà di Bruno's formula for higher-order derivatives of single-variable functions generalizes

annotations.note: Mathlib's Faà di Bruno is proven in the general Banach-space setting, covering the multivariate case.

annotations.label: Chain rule on manifolds

anchor.snippet: One generalization is to manifolds

annotations.note: The manifold chain rule is `HasMFDerivAt.comp` / `mfderiv_comp`.

annotations.label: Chain rule for Fréchet derivatives

anchor.snippet: The chain rule is also valid for Fréchet derivatives in Banach spaces

annotations.note: `HasFDerivAt.comp` is stated for Fréchet derivatives between normed/Banach spaces.

annotations.label: Chain rule for Kähler differentials

anchor.snippet: In differential algebra , the derivative is interpreted as a morphism of modules

annotations.note: Mathlib has `KaehlerDifferential.D` (the universal derivation) and `KaehlerDifferential.map` realizing functoriality, encoding the Kähler chain rule.

annotations.label: Itō's lemma

anchor.snippet: Itō's lemma , expresses the composite of an Itō process

annotations.note: Itô calculus and Itô's lemma are not present in Mathlib.
